COUNCIL MEMBERS
DAVID A. FLEMING, Mayor
ERNEST KIMME, Vice Mayor
GEORGE ADAMS
MICHAEL CONNER
DAVID LOWE

FFPC

MAY 0 2 1991
11:20 am

VACAVILLE

CITY OF VACAVILLE
650 MERCHANT STREET, VACAVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95688
ESTABLISHED 1850

May 1, 1991

John Wallace
Attorney At Law
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Letter 91-231
Vacaville-Councilman Connor

Dear Mr. Wallace,

Following upon our telephone discussion of my advice request of April
17, 1991, I am requesting a response by May 14, 1991, even should that
results in informal assistance rather than an advice letter, although I
prefer the latter.

Further, to assist you in your analysis of the situation I have prepared two
diagrams to put into focus the annexation to the City of Vacaville that
results in my request for advice.

The first diagram (marked "#1") shows the existing city in an area of
southern Vacaville. The lands within the City are shown in blue. All of
the lands shown on the diagram fall within Vacaville's sphere of influence
and long-term planning boundaries.

The second diagram (#2) shows:

In yellow: the two property owners who petitioned for annexation
to Vacaville.

In pink: the nine other property owners in the area who also
sought or did not object to annexation.

The uncolored area in the mid-right of the diagram: shows the
lands of the three property owners who objected to
annexation, one of whom is the party who is an insurance
customer of Councilman Connor.

DEPARTMENTS: Area Code (707)

Building
449-5152

City Attorney
449-5105

City Manager
449-5100

Finance
449-5117

Fire
449-1838

Personnel
449-5101

Planning
449-5140

Police
449-5200

Public Works
449-5166

Community
Services
449-5355

This pattern created an a potential "island" of unincorporated territory
which under Local Agency Formation rules are to be avoided. The
Solano County LAFCO approved the annexation of all the area in order
not to have an "island" created, thus obligating the City of Vacaville to
approve and complete the annexation of all the area by now pre-zoning
the properties of the objecting landowners. Hence, the conflict question.

If I can be of further assistance to you in your evaluation of this issue,
please feel free to contact me.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Charles O. Lamoree
City Attorney.

COUNCIL MEMBERS
DAVID A. FLEMING, Mayor
ERNEST KIMME, Vice Mayor
PAULINE CLANCY
MICHAEL CONNER
DAVID LOWE

FPPC
Apr 17 3 01 PM '91

VACAVILLE

CITY OF VACAVILLE
650 MERCHANT STREET, VACAVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95688
ESTABLISHED 1850

April 17, 1991

Office of the
City Attorney

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Advice Section
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice Letter

As City Attorney for the City of Vacaville, I am requesting an advice letter on two conflict
of interest questions under the Political Reform Act. The questions relate to a Vacaville
City Councilman, Michael Conner.

QUESTIONS PRESENTED:

First, under the facts and circumstances set forth below does Councilman Conner have a
financial conflict of interest which requires his disqualification from voting on the
prezoning of the property in question?

Second, if your advice is that a disqualifying conflict exists, may that conflict be "cured" by
waiver of all or a portion of the insurance policy commission to be received by Councilman
Conner?

FACTUAL BACKGROUND:

The City of Vacaville is seeking to annex approximately 2,000 acres of land contiguous to
its present city limits and within the City's present sphere of influence.

On April 1, LAFCO approved the annexation subject to the City prezoning the properties
prior to completion of the annexation procedures. Prezoning consists of a City Council
determination of the zoning to be applicable to property upon annexation, which prezoning
must be consistent with the provisions of the Vacaville General Plan and Zoning
Ordinance. The 2,000 acres to be annexed is held under several different ownerships.

One owner of 170 acres has their comprehensive general liability and property insurance
provided through the insurance agency of Michael Conner, a Vacaville City Councilman.
Mr. Conner is an insurance agent and has sold insurance to the property owner in question
for several years. The policy was renewed for another year on March 21, 1991. The policy
premium has been paid to the insurance company, however the agent's commission has not
yet been received by Mr. Conner. The commission from the insurance for the coming year

DEPARTMENTS: Area Code (707)

Building
449-5152

City Attorney
449-5105

City Manager
449-5100

Finance
449-5117

Fire
449-1838

Personnel
449-5101

Planning
449-5140

Police
449-5200

Public Works
449-5166

Community
Services
449-5395

[recycling symbol]
recycled paper

will be approximately $360.00, exceeding the $250.00 income limitation under the Political
Reform Act.

The property in question is subdivided into seven lots. Six of which are about 20 acres in
size and one of which is 43 acres in size. The zoning under the County General Plan and
Zoning Ordinance is "A-20", an agricultural designation allowing subdivision into 20 acre
lots.

The zoning designation applicable under the City after annexation would also be "A-20"
pursuant to both the provisions of the Vacaville General Plan which designates the area as
open space with a zoning to be allowed at the "A-20" level. Further, under the Vacaville
Zoning Ordinance "...all territory which annexed to the City or which is unzoned or
becomes unzoned through abandonment of public street or railroad rights-of-way, shall be
classified in the A-20 (zoning) district." See Vacaville Municipal Code Section 17.78.110.

Thus, the zoning designations for the property in question under the County and City
General Plan and Zoning Ordinance are identical.

However, the development standards applicable in the City are different from those
applicable in the County. For example, the County allows wells for domestic water usage.
The City, on the other hand, would require use of City water either through a direct
connection to the City water distribution system if a building one was within a water "zone"
or through a private water system consisting of City water pumped to holding tanks to
supply water to building sites above a City water "zone".

Thus, the development potential in terms of the number of lots available is identical
between the County and the City, but the development standards applicable in the City are
different than those within the County. Therefore, the costs associated with development
of a parcel and the location of building sites on individual parcels may be different from
those required of the property developer weren't in the County. Unless site specific civil
and structural engineering studies are undertaken on each lot, it is impossible to tell the
value implications between the development standards of the City and the County.

ANALYSIS OF THE ISSUES:

Utilizing the four-part test of conflicts of interest under FPPC analysis, it is clear that (1)
Councilman Conner is involved in a governmental decision; to wit: the prezoning of the
property in question as a condition of the annexation of the property; and (2) that more
than $250.00 would be received by Councilman Conner as a commission from the
insurance policy for the property.

Conflict analysis however requires that the effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest be "material". Under Title 2, California Code of Regulations, Section 18702.1(a)(1) the effect of a decision is material if a person who has been a source of more than
$250.00 of income to the public official is directly involved in a decision before the public
official's agency. Up to this point of the analysis it would appear that Councilman Conner
would have to disqualify himself. However, under subsection (c) of Section 18702.1,
disqualification, otherwise required under subsection (a)(1) would not be required if the
decision would have no financial effect on the "...person or business entity appearing before
the official, or on the real property."

In this circumstance I believe that the appropriate analysis would be that there would be no
financial impact on the real property because the zoning is identical between the County
General Plan and Zoning Standards and the City's Zoning and General Plan Standards.

2

The economic differentiation between the development standards applicable in the City
and those in the County forms the only basis of distinction between the property if annexed.
Under Section 18702.1(a)(3)(e) an interest in real property which would otherwise be
disqualifying due to change in valuation is not disqualifying if the change in the value arises
out of a "...an amendment of an existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulations
(such as changes in the uses permitted, or development standards applicable, within a
particular zoning category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that
category." That is exactly the situation we have here since the City's development
standards are applicable to any "A-20" zoning designation within the City and are
"...applicable to all other properties designated in that category." Thus I believe there is no
basis for the finding of the "materially" component of an FPPC evaluation.

Further, the final component of a conflicts analysis is whether the effect of the decision on
the economic interest is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. Since the
zoning designations are identical and the development standards are applicable to any
property within the City zoned "A-20", I do not believe that the decision in this case would
be distinguishable from that of the effect on the public generally and thus the forth
component of a conflict analysis would not be present either.

Finally, Councilman Conner has not yet received the commission from the insurance policy
in question and is willing to waive that portion of the insurance commission which puts him
over the $250,000 income limitation. This would result in a return of that portion of the
commission to the property owner. Such a "curing" of conflicts is allowable under a
campaign contribution analysis. Likewise, I would request your advice on the question of
whether Councilman Conner can waive the excess commission from this insurance policy
and thus not fall within any political reform act prohibition from participation in this
prezoning as part of the annexation process.

I would appreciate a prompt response to this advice request. The matter is set for hearing
before the City Council on May 14, 1991. If you answer within 21 days as provided in your
regulations, there would be sufficient time to receive your advice on this matter prior to the
Council meeting at which the issue will be addressed.

If I can be of further assistance to you in your analysis of this matter, please contact me at
(707) 449-5105.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

CHARLES O. LAMOREE
City Attorney

sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Councilman Michael Conner has a disqualifying financial conflict of interest regarding his vote on the prezoning of property owned by a party who is his insurance customer, and whether such conflict can be cured by waiving the insurance commission.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City Attorney to FPPC, dated April 17, 1991. The May 1 follow-up letter is also included requesting informal assistance by May 14. Document contains detailed factual background and legal analysis by the requesting party. OCR quality is poor in places but questions and context are clear. No FPPC response/conclusion is present in this document.

Second, if your advice is that a disqualifying conflict exists, may that conflict be "cured" by waiver of all or a portion of the insurance policy commission to be received by Councilman Conner?

embedding.summary: City Attorney Charles O. Lamoree of Vacaville requests an FPPC advice letter regarding whether Councilman Michael Conner must recuse himself from voting on the prezoning of approximately 2,000 acres scheduled for annexation, given that Conner's insurance agency serves one of the property owners and will receive approximately $360 in commission (exceeding the $250 threshold). The request also asks whether waiving the excess commission would cure any conflict.
